subject: Re: Tomogram reconstruction - unbinned tomogram dimension
body:
Dear Alister and Dimitry,

Ah ok, now I got it :)

And now it works!
Thanks a lot for your help and explanations!

Best
Katarina

sri, 2. pro 2020. u 13:38 Alister Burt <alisterburt@gmail.com> napisao je:

> Hi Katarina,
>
> The pixelX/Y parameters should be the pixel size of the images you are
> working with in Warp rather than the desired pixel size. This is most
> likely the unbinned pixel size (unless you did some binning in Warp at some
> stage)
>
> The reason it’s taking so long is that you are telling it the unbinned
> pixel size is 10A then asking it to also reconstruct at 10A, therefore
> trying to generate an unbinned reconstruction
>
> As an aside, all images are downscaled by cropping in Fourier space rather
> than real space binning - this means you can reconstruct at any pixel size,
> not just multiples of the unbinned pixel size
>
> Cheers,
>
> Alister
>
> On 2 Dec 2020, at 13:28, Katarina Belačić <katarina.belacic@gmail.com>
> wrote:
>
> ﻿
> Hi Dimitry,
>
> Thanks for the clarification, this now makes sense.
> I did try as you suggested (I hope that's what you meant) - I just put my
> desired pixel size in Pixel X/Y (10.72) and in Bin: 0.00x (10.72 Å/pxl).
> Also, I switched dimensions: original 5760x4092 and in Unbinned tomogram
> dimensions, I put 4092 x 5760 x 1600.
>
> But when I started preprocessing it just ran forever (4+ hours) which
> seemed odd to me.
> Is it normal that it takes forever to preprocess with these settings or
> did I put something wrong?
>
> Best
> Katarina
>
>
> uto, 1. pro 2020. u 12:57 teg...@gmail.com <tegunov@gmail.com> napisao je:
>
>> Hi Katarina,
>>
>> There seem to be 2 issues here:
>>
>> The definition of "binning" is different between Warp and IMOD. In IMOD
>> it's a scaling factor (size_binned = size_original / binning), in Warp it's
>> the exponent of the scaling factor (size_binned = size_original /
>> 2^binning). The rationale being "if you bin once, the size is cut in half;
>> if you bin twice, the size is cut in half twice". Thus, IMOD's binning = 1
>> is Warp's binning = 0, and binning = 2 is Warp's binning = 1. This is why
>> Warp's reconstructed volume is 2x smaller than IMOD's in your screenshot
>> (notice the different zoom factors). Unless your raw data were collected
>> with super-resolution, I'd leave the binning parameter in Warp's input
>> options at 0, and adjust the desired pixel size in the tomogram
>> reconstruction dialog.
>>
>> Another issue is that the tomogram dimensions are defined in volume
>> space, which sees the tilt images already rotated by the ca. 90° rotation
>> of the original tilt axis. Thus, if your K3 images are 6k x 4k, the volume
>> dimensions should be 4k x 6k.
>>
>> Cheers,
>> Dimitry
>>
>> On Tuesday, December 1, 2020 at 11:49:01 AM UTC+1 katarina...@gmail.com
>> wrote:
>>
>>> Hello Dimitry,
>>>
>>> I am confused with the input option for tomogram reconstruction -
>>> unbinned tomogram dimension.
>>>
>>> When I put in the original tomogram dimensions (unbinned) the result
>>> reconstruction looks somewhat magnified and cut on the sides when compared
>>> to IMOD reconstruction (attachment - left IMOD reconstruction, right Warp
>>> reconstruction).
>>>
>>> I used 2x binning in both Warp and IMOD.
>>>
>>> What is the value I need to put in here actually to get the whole
>>> tomogram?
>>>
>>> Best
>>> Katarina
>>>
>>> --
